title: Soft-Scrambled Eggs on Buttered Toast

ingredients.name: butter
visual_checks: divided — 1 tbsp for eggs, 1 tbsp to spread on toast
ingredients.notes: divided — 1 tbsp for eggs, 1 tbsp to spread on toast

subs.name: ghee — richer flavour and a higher smoke point
sourdough, whole-wheat pav, or leftover chapatti — all make good bases

ingredients.name: fresh cream
visual_checks: adds extra lusciousness; skip for a lighter result
ingredients.notes: adds extra lusciousness; skip for a lighter result

subs.name: milk or a teaspoon of curd stirred in at the end
subs.notes: milk gives a lighter result; curd adds a gentle tang

steps.text: Toast the bread in a pan or under a grill until golden on both sides. Spread one tablespoon of butter generously over each slice while still hot, then set aside on warmed plates.

Crack the eggs into a bowl, add the cream if using, season with salt and pepper, and beat gently with a fork until just combined — you want streaks of yolk visible, not a uniform mixture. Over-beating makes the eggs tough.

Place the remaining butter in a small non-stick saucepan or frying pan over the lowest possible flame. Pour in the egg mixture and begin stirring slowly with a wooden spoon or silicone spatula, moving it across the entire base of the pan in lazy figure-of-eight strokes.

Every 20–30 seconds lift the pan off the heat entirely for a few seconds while continuing to stir — this prevents the eggs from overcooking. The whole process takes 4–5 minutes and the eggs should finish in large, glossy, barely-set curds that hold their shape.

Remove from the heat while the eggs still look slightly underdone — residual heat will finish the job as you plate. Spoon immediately over the buttered toast, scatter spring onion greens on top and serve at once.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: cream → milk or a teaspoon of curd stirred in at the end (milk gives a lighter result; curd adds a gentle tang); butter → ghee — richer flavour and a higher smoke point (use the same quantity); bread → sourdough, whole-wheat pav, or leftover chapatti — all make good bases (chapatti adds a pleasant chewiness)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Any heavy-based non-stick pan or tawa works perfectly. Use Amul butter and Britannia or local thick-sliced bread. The low-heat method suits Indian gas burners well — keep the knob to minimum. A pinch of turmeric or a slit green chilli added with the eggs gives a desi twist without overwhelming the dish.
